title: Data Analytics
description: Data analysts are the storytellers of the tech world. You take messy, raw data and turn it into clear insights that help companies make smarter decisions. Think of it like being a detective — you dig through numbers to find patterns, trends, and answers that nobody else can see. Every company with data (which is every company) needs people who can make sense of it.
whatYoullActuallyDo: Pull data from databases using SQL to answer business questions
Clean and organize messy datasets so they're usable
Build dashboards and charts in Tableau or Power BI that executives actually look at
Write reports that explain what the data means in plain English
Collaborate with product, marketing, and engineering teams to define what to measure
Spot trends and anomalies — like why did sales drop 20% last Tuesday?

aiSkills.name: Prompt Engineering for SQL Queries
aiSkills.description: Use ChatGPT or Claude to write, debug, and optimize SQL queries. Instead of memorizing every SQL function, you describe what you need in plain English and the AI writes the query for you.

aiSkills.name: AI-Powered Data Cleaning
aiSkills.description: Use tools like Code Interpreter or Claude to automatically find and fix messy data — missing values, duplicates, wrong formats. What used to take hours now takes minutes.

aiSkills.name: Automated Visualization Generation
aiSkills.description: Describe the chart you want in words, and AI tools generate the Python/R code to create it. You focus on the story, not the syntax.

aiSkills.name: Natural Language Data Exploration
aiSkills.description: Ask questions about your data in plain English using AI tools. 'What were our top 5 products last quarter?' gets you an instant answer without writing a single query.

aiSkills.name: Predictive Modeling with AI Assistance
aiSkills.description: Use AI to build basic forecasting models. You don't need a PhD in statistics — AI helps you pick the right model, train it, and interpret the results.

aiSkills.name: Automated Report Generation
aiSkills.description: Use AI to turn raw analysis into polished written reports and executive summaries. Feed it your findings and it drafts the narrative.

transferableSkills.name: Attention to Detail
transferableSkills.description: Your ability to spot errors and inconsistencies is exactly what data cleaning requires. Accountants who catch a $0.01 discrepancy make excellent data analysts.

transferableSkills.name: Storytelling & Communication
transferableSkills.description: The best analysts aren't just number-crunchers — they explain findings so anyone can understand. Your ability to present information clearly is a superpower.

transferableSkills.name: Problem Solving
transferableSkills.description: Every day you've solved problems at work — figuring out schedules, resolving customer issues, fixing processes. Analytics is just problem solving with data.

transferableSkills.name: Critical Thinking
transferableSkills.description: Questioning assumptions and evaluating evidence is at the heart of data analysis. If you've ever asked 'but does this actually make sense?', you think like an analyst.

transferableSkills.name: Business Acumen
transferableSkills.description: Understanding how a business works helps you ask the right questions. Technical analysts often lack this — it's your competitive edge.

roadmap.title: Build Your Foundation
roadmap.description: Learn the core tools every analyst uses on day one. Focus on getting comfortable, not perfect.
steps: Learn SQL basics — SELECT, WHERE, JOIN, GROUP BY (this is the #1 skill employers look for)
Complete Google Data Analytics Certificate on Coursera (free with trial)
Set up a free Tableau Public account and follow along with 3 guided tutorials
Learn spreadsheet fundamentals — pivot tables, VLOOKUP, conditional formatting in Google Sheets or Excel
Practice with real datasets from Kaggle's beginner-friendly datasets

roadmap.title: Level Up Your Skills
roadmap.description: Go deeper on SQL, start learning Python, and begin building things you can show employers.
steps: Intermediate SQL — subqueries, window functions, CTEs (Common Table Expressions)
Learn Python basics — focus on pandas library for data manipulation
Build your first dashboard in Tableau using a public dataset (e.g., Airbnb listings, COVID data)
Learn basic statistics — mean, median, standard deviation, correlation (Khan Academy is free)
Practice AI-assisted analysis: use ChatGPT/Claude to help write and debug SQL and Python code
Start a portfolio on GitHub — even simple projects count

roadmap.title: Build Real Projects
roadmap.description: Create 2-3 portfolio projects that prove you can do the job. These are what get you interviews.
steps: Project 1: Clean a messy dataset, analyze it, and present findings in a Tableau dashboard
Project 2: Use SQL to answer 5+ business questions from a realistic database (try Mode Analytics' free SQL tutorial)
Project 3: Build an end-to-end analysis — collect data, clean it, analyze it, visualize it, write up findings
Write a blog post or LinkedIn article explaining one of your projects in plain English
Get feedback on your portfolio from r/dataanalysis or analytics Discord communities

roadmap.title: Get Hired
roadmap.description: Time to land the job. Focus on applications, networking, and interview prep.
steps: Polish your LinkedIn — headline should say 'Data Analyst' not 'Aspiring Data Analyst'
Apply to 5-10 jobs per week — focus on 'Junior Data Analyst' and 'Business Analyst' roles
Practice SQL interview questions on StrataScratch or LeetCode (SQL section)
Prepare for case study interviews — practice explaining your analysis process out loud
Network: attend local data meetups, comment on LinkedIn posts, reach out to analysts for coffee chats
Apply to apprenticeship programs listed below (Google, Microsoft Leap)
Consider contract/freelance analytics work on Upwork to build real experience

freeResources.name: Google Data Analytics Certificate
freeResources.description: The gold standard free certificate. Covers spreadsheets, SQL, Tableau, and R. Takes about 6 months part-time.

freeResources.name: SQLBolt
freeResources.description: Learn SQL through hands-on, interactive exercises. No setup required — runs in your browser.

freeResources.name: Kaggle Learn
freeResources.description: Free micro-courses on Python, Pandas, Data Visualization, and more. Practical and project-based.

freeResources.name: Tableau Public Resources
freeResources.description: Free version of Tableau with tons of guided tutorials. Build dashboards and publish them to your portfolio.

freeResources.name: Khan Academy – Statistics
freeResources.description: Free, well-explained statistics fundamentals. Covers everything you need for entry-level analytics.

freeResources.name: Mode SQL Tutorial
freeResources.description: Practice SQL on real-world datasets. Great for intermediate skills and interview prep.

title: Software Engineering
description: Software engineers build the apps, websites, and systems that millions of people use every day. You write code to create new features, squash bugs, and make things run faster. The best part? With AI tools like Copilot and Claude, you don't need to memorize every syntax detail — you need to think logically, break problems into smaller pieces, and communicate clearly with your team. This is the most accessible it's ever been to become a software engineer.
whatYoullActuallyDo: Write code in languages like JavaScript, Python, or TypeScript to build features
Review your teammates' code and have yours reviewed (called 'pull requests')
Debug issues — figure out why something broke and fix it
Participate in daily standups and sprint planning with your team
Write tests to make sure your code works correctly
Collaborate with designers and product managers to turn ideas into working software

aiSkills.name: AI-Assisted Coding with Copilot/Cursor
aiSkills.description: Use AI coding assistants that autocomplete your code, suggest entire functions, and help you write code faster. It's like having a senior developer pair-programming with you 24/7.

aiSkills.name: Debugging with LLMs
aiSkills.description: Paste error messages into Claude or ChatGPT and get plain-English explanations of what went wrong and how to fix it. No more staring at cryptic stack traces for hours.

aiSkills.name: AI-Generated Unit Tests
aiSkills.description: Let AI write your test cases. Describe what your function should do, and AI generates comprehensive tests that catch edge cases you might miss.

aiSkills.name: Code Refactoring with AI
aiSkills.description: Ask AI to improve your code's readability, performance, or structure. It's like having a code review from a senior engineer on demand.

aiSkills.name: Learning New Frameworks with AI
aiSkills.description: When you need to learn React, Next.js, or any new technology, AI can explain concepts, generate starter code, and walk you through tutorials tailored to your level.

aiSkills.name: AI-Powered Documentation
aiSkills.description: Generate clear documentation and code comments automatically. AI reads your code and explains what it does, making your work easier for others to understand.

transferableSkills.name: Logic and Reasoning
transferableSkills.description: Programming is logic in action. If you can follow a recipe, troubleshoot a car problem, or build IKEA furniture from instructions, you can learn to code.

transferableSkills.name: Project Management
transferableSkills.description: Breaking large tasks into smaller ones, estimating timelines, and coordinating with others — you've been doing software project management without knowing it.

transferableSkills.name: Creativity
transferableSkills.description: Great software isn't just functional — it's elegant. Creative problem-solving helps you find solutions others miss and build things people love to use.

transferableSkills.name: Continuous Learning
transferableSkills.description: Tech changes fast, but so does every industry. Your ability to learn new skills and adapt is more valuable than any single programming language.

transferableSkills.name: Communication
transferableSkills.description: Writing clear code is like writing clear English. Explaining technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders is a skill most engineers struggle with — and you already have it.

roadmap.title: Learn to Code
roadmap.description: Pick ONE language and stick with it. JavaScript is recommended because it works for both websites and servers.
steps: Complete freeCodeCamp's Responsive Web Design certification (HTML + CSS)
Learn JavaScript fundamentals — variables, functions, loops, arrays, objects
Build 3 small projects: a calculator, a to-do list, and a quiz app
Set up VS Code with GitHub Copilot (free for students) or Cursor
Learn Git basics — clone, commit, push, pull, branch (this is how all teams collaborate)
Create a GitHub account and push your projects there

roadmap.title: Build with Frameworks
roadmap.description: Learn a modern framework that employers actually hire for. React is the most popular choice.
steps: Learn React fundamentals — components, props, state, hooks
Build a full project with React (e.g., a weather app that calls a real API)
Learn basic backend concepts — REST APIs, HTTP methods, JSON
Try Node.js + Express to build a simple API
Learn to use databases — start with a simple one like SQLite or use a service like Supabase
Practice using AI assistants daily: ask Copilot to write functions, then understand what it wrote

roadmap.title: Build Portfolio Projects
roadmap.description: Build 2-3 impressive projects that solve real problems. Quality over quantity.
steps: Project 1: A full-stack app with user authentication (e.g., a notes app, budget tracker)
Project 2: An app that uses an external API (e.g., movie search, recipe finder, weather dashboard)
Project 3: A collaborative or unique project that shows personality (e.g., a game, a tool for your hobby)
Deploy all projects so they're live on the internet (use Vercel or Netlify — both free)
Write a README for each project explaining what it does, what you learned, and how to run it
Contribute to one open-source project on GitHub (even fixing a typo in docs counts)

roadmap.title: Get Hired
roadmap.description: Apply strategically and prepare for technical interviews.
steps: Build a personal portfolio website showcasing your projects
Practice coding challenges on LeetCode (Easy level) — focus on arrays, strings, and hashmaps
Apply to apprenticeship programs (Google, Microsoft Leap, LinkedIn REACH, Airbnb Connect)
Apply to 10+ junior developer roles per week
Practice behavioral interview questions using the STAR method
Do mock technical interviews with friends or on Pramp (free)
Network on Twitter/X and LinkedIn — share what you're building and learning
Attend local meetups and hackathons

freeResources.name: freeCodeCamp
freeResources.description: The best free, comprehensive coding curriculum. Covers HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, Node.js, and more with hands-on projects.

freeResources.name: The Odin Project
freeResources.description: A free, open-source curriculum that teaches full-stack web development with real-world projects. Very thorough.

freeResources.name: JavaScript.info
freeResources.description: The modern JavaScript tutorial. Clear explanations with interactive examples. Bookmark this — you'll use it constantly.

freeResources.name: CS50 by Harvard
freeResources.description: Harvard's legendary intro to computer science. Free on YouTube/edX. Gives you the fundamentals that make everything else click.

freeResources.name: Scrimba
freeResources.description: Interactive coding screencasts where you can pause and edit the instructor's code. Many free courses on React and JavaScript.

freeResources.name: LeetCode (Easy Problems)
freeResources.description: Practice coding interview questions. Start with Easy difficulty. Focus on the top 50 most-asked questions.

title: Program Management
description: Program managers are the glue that holds tech teams together. You don't write code — you make sure the right things get built, on time, and without chaos. You run meetings, track progress, remove blockers, and communicate between engineering, design, and leadership. If you've ever organized a complex event, managed a team, or coordinated anything involving multiple people and deadlines, you already have the core skills for this role.
whatYoullActuallyDo: Run sprint planning meetings and daily standups with engineering teams
Create and maintain project timelines, roadmaps, and status documents
Identify risks early and create mitigation plans before things go wrong
Communicate project status to leadership and stakeholders in clear, concise updates
Remove blockers — if an engineer is stuck waiting on another team, you make the call or send the email
Write project specifications and requirements documents that engineering teams build from

aiSkills.name: AI-Powered Meeting Summaries
aiSkills.description: Use AI tools to automatically summarize meeting recordings into action items, decisions, and follow-ups. Never miss an important detail from a 1-hour meeting again.

aiSkills.name: Drafting Project Specs with AI
aiSkills.description: Describe a feature in plain language and have AI generate a structured project specification — complete with requirements, success metrics, edge cases, and timeline estimates.

aiSkills.name: Risk Assessment Analysis
aiSkills.description: Use AI to analyze project plans and identify potential risks, dependencies, and bottlenecks you might have missed. It's like having a second brain reviewing your strategy.

aiSkills.name: Stakeholder Communication Drafting
aiSkills.description: Generate polished status updates, executive summaries, and stakeholder emails. AI helps you strike the right tone — detailed for engineers, concise for executives.

aiSkills.name: Data-Driven Decision Support
aiSkills.description: Use AI to analyze project metrics, velocity data, and resource allocation to make better planning decisions backed by numbers.

aiSkills.name: Process Documentation
aiSkills.description: Have AI help create clear process documents, runbooks, and onboarding guides. What took a day to write now takes an hour.

transferableSkills.name: Organization & Planning
transferableSkills.description: Managing timelines, coordinating people, and keeping track of a hundred moving pieces? That's literally the job description of a program manager.

transferableSkills.name: Communication
transferableSkills.description: PMs spend 70% of their time communicating. Your ability to explain complex things simply and rally people around a goal is the most valuable skill in this role.

transferableSkills.name: Leadership Without Authority
transferableSkills.description: PMs don't manage people directly — they influence through persuasion, trust, and clarity. If you've ever led a project without being the boss, you know this skill.

transferableSkills.name: Strategic Planning
transferableSkills.description: Thinking long-term while executing short-term. Balancing competing priorities. Knowing when to say no. These strategic instincts transfer directly.

transferableSkills.name: Conflict Resolution
transferableSkills.description: When engineering wants to rebuild the whole system and sales wants the feature yesterday, the PM finds the middle ground. Your mediation skills are gold.

roadmap.title: Learn the Language of Tech
roadmap.description: You don't need to code, but you need to understand how software gets built. Learn enough to be dangerous.
steps: Learn Agile/Scrum methodology — take the free Scrum.org learning path
Understand the software development lifecycle (SDLC): planning, development, testing, deployment
Learn basic tech vocabulary: API, frontend, backend, database, deployment, sprint, standup
Get familiar with tools: Jira, Asana, or Linear (project tracking), Confluence or Notion (documentation)
Read 'Inspired' by Marty Cagan — the bible of product/program management in tech
Watch day-in-the-life videos of TPMs at Google, Amazon, and Meta on YouTube

roadmap.title: Get Certified & Practice
roadmap.description: Build credentials and start applying PM thinking to real scenarios.
steps: Get your Google Project Management Certificate (Coursera, free with trial)
Study for and pass the PSM I (Professional Scrum Master) exam ($150 — worth it for your resume)
Practice writing project specs — pick any app feature and write the requirements document
Learn SQL basics (just SELECT/WHERE/JOIN) — PMs who can query data stand out
Practice stakeholder communication: write 5 mock status update emails for different audiences
Join PM communities: Lenny's Newsletter, The Product Folks, r/projectmanagement

roadmap.title: Build Your PM Portfolio
roadmap.description: Create tangible proof that you can do the job, even without the title.
steps: Create a case study: pick a real app, identify a problem, and write a full project plan to fix it
Volunteer to manage a project — local nonprofit, open source project, or community event
Document everything: timelines you created, meetings you ran, problems you solved
Build a simple portfolio website or Notion page showcasing your case studies
Practice using AI tools for your PM workflow: meeting summaries, spec writing, risk analysis

roadmap.title: Get Hired
roadmap.description: PMs get hired through networking as much as applications. Do both.
steps: Apply for 'Technical Program Manager', 'Project Manager', and 'Program Coordinator' roles
Search for TPM apprenticeships (Google, Microsoft Leap)
Practice PM interview questions: 'Tell me about a time you managed conflicting priorities'
Practice case study interviews: 'How would you launch feature X?'
Network aggressively — reach out to PMs at target companies on LinkedIn for informational interviews
Highlight your transferable experience: 'I managed X people, Y budget, Z timelines in my previous role'
Consider roles like 'Scrum Master' or 'Project Coordinator' as stepping stones into tech

freeResources.name: Google Project Management Certificate
freeResources.description: Comprehensive project management course from Google. Covers Agile, Scrum, stakeholder management, and risk planning. Free with Coursera trial.

freeResources.name: Scrum.org Open Assessments
freeResources.description: Free practice assessments for Scrum methodology. Great way to test your knowledge and prep for PSM I certification.

freeResources.name: Lenny's Newsletter
freeResources.description: The most-read newsletter in product and program management. Real-world advice from PMs at top companies. Free tier available.

freeResources.name: Product School (Free Talks)
freeResources.description: Free webinars and talks from PMs at Google, Meta, Amazon. Great for learning what the job actually looks like day-to-day.

freeResources.name: Atlassian Agile Coach
freeResources.description: Free comprehensive guide to Agile methodology from the makers of Jira. Covers Scrum, Kanban, sprint planning, and more.

freeResources.name: SQL for PMs (Mode Analytics)
freeResources.description: Free SQL tutorial. PMs who can pull their own data don't have to wait on analysts. Learn just enough SQL to be self-sufficient.

title: Marketing
description: Tech marketers help amazing products find the people who need them. You blend creativity with data — writing compelling copy, running campaigns, analyzing what works, and telling a brand's story across channels. With AI tools, one marketer can now do what used to take a whole team. If you've ever convinced someone to try something new, written something that made people feel something, or obsessed over why one Instagram post did better than another — marketing in tech is your lane.
whatYoullActuallyDo: Write copy for websites, emails, ads, and social media that converts browsers into customers
Run and analyze paid ad campaigns on Google, Meta, LinkedIn, and TikTok
Track marketing metrics — click-through rates, conversion rates, cost per acquisition
Create content strategies: blog posts, newsletters, video scripts, and social media calendars
Collaborate with product and design teams on launch campaigns for new features
Use tools like Google Analytics, HubSpot, and Mailchimp to measure and optimize performance

aiSkills.name: AI Content Generation & Ideation
aiSkills.description: Use AI to brainstorm campaign ideas, write first drafts of blog posts, generate social media copy variations, and overcome writer's block. You edit and add the human touch — AI handles the heavy lifting.

aiSkills.name: SEO Optimization with AI
aiSkills.description: Use AI tools to research keywords, optimize page titles and meta descriptions, analyze competitor content, and generate SEO-friendly content outlines. Rank higher with less guesswork.

aiSkills.name: Customer Sentiment Analysis
aiSkills.description: Feed customer reviews, social mentions, and survey responses into AI to instantly understand how people feel about your product. Spot trends and concerns before they become problems.

aiSkills.name: Personalized Campaign Drafting
aiSkills.description: Use AI to create multiple versions of emails, ads, and landing pages tailored to different audience segments. What used to require an agency now takes an afternoon.

aiSkills.name: AI-Powered Analytics Interpretation
aiSkills.description: Paste your Google Analytics or campaign data into AI and ask 'what's working and what's not?' Get instant analysis and actionable recommendations.

aiSkills.name: Visual Content Creation with AI
aiSkills.description: Use AI image generators and design tools to create social media graphics, ad mockups, and presentation visuals. Perfect for marketers who aren't designers.

transferableSkills.name: Communication & Writing
transferableSkills.description: If you can write a clear email, a compelling story, or a persuasive argument, you can write marketing copy. This is the single most important skill in marketing.

transferableSkills.name: Psychology & Empathy
transferableSkills.description: Understanding why people make decisions is the foundation of marketing. Your ability to put yourself in someone else's shoes directly translates to creating messages that resonate.

transferableSkills.name: Data Interpretation
transferableSkills.description: Modern marketing is data-driven. If you can read a spreadsheet and spot trends, you can analyze campaign performance and make optimization decisions.

transferableSkills.name: Creativity & Design Sense
transferableSkills.description: Creating visually appealing content, understanding brand aesthetics, and thinking outside the box are what separate good marketers from great ones.

transferableSkills.name: Relationship Building
transferableSkills.description: Marketing is about building relationships at scale. Your networking instincts and ability to build rapport translate perfectly to brand building and community engagement.

roadmap.title: Learn the Marketing Stack
roadmap.description: Get familiar with the tools and concepts every tech marketer uses.
steps: Complete Google Digital Marketing & E-commerce Certificate (Coursera, free with trial)
Set up Google Analytics on a personal website or blog (even a free WordPress site works)
Learn email marketing basics with Mailchimp's free tier — build a small email list and send a campaign
Study the marketing funnel: awareness, consideration, conversion, retention
Follow marketing leaders on LinkedIn and Twitter/X: Seth Godin, Rand Fishkin, Amanda Natividad
Read 'Everybody Writes' by Ann Handley — the essential guide to content marketing

roadmap.title: Specialize & Build Skills
roadmap.description: Pick a marketing specialty and go deep. You'll stand out more as a specialist than a generalist.
steps: Choose a specialty: Content Marketing, Growth/Performance Marketing, Product Marketing, or Social Media
Learn SEO fundamentals — keyword research, on-page optimization, link building (Moz and Ahrefs have free courses)
Master one ad platform: Google Ads or Meta Ads Manager (both have free certification programs)
Learn to use AI tools in your daily workflow: content drafts, A/B test ideas, audience research
Study copywriting frameworks: AIDA (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action), PAS (Problem, Agitate, Solution)
Analyze 5 marketing campaigns from tech companies you admire — what worked and why?

roadmap.title: Build Your Portfolio
roadmap.description: Create real marketing work you can show in interviews. Results speak louder than resumes.
steps: Start a blog or newsletter on a topic you're passionate about — this IS your portfolio
Run a small paid ad campaign (even $50 on Meta) and document the results and learnings
Create a mock marketing campaign for a real product: strategy, copy, visuals, metrics plan
Write 3-5 sample pieces: blog post, email sequence, social media campaign, landing page copy
Build a simple portfolio website showcasing your campaigns, writing, and results
Offer free marketing help to a local business or nonprofit to get real-world results

roadmap.title: Get Hired
roadmap.description: Tech companies are always hiring marketers. Position yourself right and the interviews will come.
steps: Apply for roles: 'Marketing Coordinator', 'Content Marketing Associate', 'Growth Marketing Analyst'
Target startups — they often care more about skills and portfolio than years of experience
Prepare for marketing interviews: be ready to present a campaign idea or audit a landing page live
Build your personal brand on LinkedIn — share marketing insights, case studies, and learnings weekly
Network with tech marketers — most marketing roles are filled through referrals
Consider freelance marketing work on Upwork or Fiverr to build client experience and testimonials
Apply to Google's marketing apprenticeship and other tech company early-career programs

freeResources.name: Google Digital Marketing Certificate
freeResources.description: Google's comprehensive digital marketing course. Covers SEO, SEM, social media, email, and analytics. Free with Coursera trial.

freeResources.name: HubSpot Academy
freeResources.description: Free certifications in inbound marketing, content marketing, email marketing, and social media. Recognized across the industry.

freeResources.name: Google Ads Certification
freeResources.description: Free certification directly from Google. Proves you can run paid search and display campaigns. A resume must-have.

freeResources.name: Moz SEO Learning Center
freeResources.description: The most-referenced SEO guide on the internet. Free, comprehensive, and updated regularly.

freeResources.name: Copyblogger
freeResources.description: The gold standard for learning copywriting and content marketing. Years of free, actionable advice.

freeResources.name: Canva Design School
freeResources.description: Free design tutorials and templates. Learn to create professional-looking marketing visuals without being a graphic designer.

title: UX/UI Design
description: UX/UI designers make technology feel human. You research how people actually use products, then design interfaces that are intuitive, beautiful, and accessible. It's the perfect blend of creativity and problem-solving — you're part psychologist, part artist, part detective. If you've ever been frustrated by a confusing app and thought 'I could make this better,' this is your career. With tools like Figma and AI-powered design assistants, the barrier to entry has never been lower.
whatYoullActuallyDo: Research user needs through interviews, surveys, and usability testing
Create wireframes and prototypes in Figma to test ideas before engineers build them
Design polished, pixel-perfect interfaces for web and mobile apps
Build and maintain design systems — reusable component libraries that keep products consistent
Collaborate with product managers and engineers to turn requirements into intuitive experiences
Present design decisions to stakeholders and iterate based on feedback and data

aiSkills.name: AI-Powered Design Generation
aiSkills.description: Use tools like Midjourney, DALL-E, and Figma AI to rapidly generate design concepts, mood boards, and UI variations. Explore 10 directions in the time it used to take to sketch one.

aiSkills.name: AI User Research Analysis
aiSkills.description: Feed interview transcripts and survey responses into AI to identify patterns, pain points, and opportunities. Synthesize hours of research data in minutes.

aiSkills.name: AI-Assisted Copywriting for UI
aiSkills.description: Use AI to write clear, concise microcopy — button labels, error messages, onboarding flows. Good UX writing is invisible, and AI helps you get it right.

aiSkills.name: Automated Accessibility Audits
aiSkills.description: Use AI tools to scan your designs for accessibility issues — color contrast, screen reader compatibility, keyboard navigation. Build inclusive products from the start.

aiSkills.name: Rapid Prototyping with AI
aiSkills.description: Describe an interaction in plain English and have AI generate prototype code or Figma layouts. Test ideas with users faster than ever before.

aiSkills.name: AI Design System Management
aiSkills.description: Use AI to audit design systems for inconsistencies, generate component documentation, and suggest improvements. Keep your design language clean and scalable.

transferableSkills.name: Empathy & Listening
transferableSkills.description: UX design is fundamentally about understanding people. Your ability to listen, observe, and put yourself in someone else's shoes is the most important UX skill there is.

transferableSkills.name: Visual & Spatial Thinking
transferableSkills.description: If you think in images, layouts, and spatial relationships, you already have a designer's brain. Translating ideas into visual form is what UI design is all about.

transferableSkills.name: Storytelling
transferableSkills.description: Great UX tells a story — guiding users from point A to point B. Your ability to craft a narrative translates directly to designing user flows and journeys.

transferableSkills.name: Research & Analysis
transferableSkills.description: UX research is just research with a different subject. If you know how to form hypotheses, gather data, and draw conclusions, you can do user research.

transferableSkills.name: Attention to Detail
transferableSkills.description: The difference between good and great design is in the details — spacing, alignment, consistency. Your eye for catching what's off makes you a natural UI designer.

roadmap.title: Learn Design Fundamentals
roadmap.description: Master the tools and principles every designer uses. Figma is free and the industry standard.
steps: Learn Figma basics — frames, auto-layout, components, and prototyping (Figma's own tutorials are excellent)
Study core design principles: hierarchy, contrast, alignment, proximity, repetition
Complete Google UX Design Certificate on Coursera (free with trial)
Learn color theory, typography basics, and spacing/grid systems
Study 5 well-designed apps (like Airbnb, Stripe, Linear) — analyze why they feel good to use
Start a design journal: screenshot UI patterns you love and explain what works

roadmap.title: Learn UX Research & Process
roadmap.description: Great design starts with understanding users. Learn the research methods that inform every design decision.
steps: Learn user research methods: interviews, surveys, usability testing, card sorting
Practice creating user personas, journey maps, and empathy maps
Learn information architecture — how to organize content so people can find what they need
Study interaction design patterns — navigation, forms, modals, notifications
Learn wireframing: start with low-fidelity sketches before jumping to high-fidelity designs
Run a usability test on an existing product with 3-5 people (friends and family count)

roadmap.title: Build Your Portfolio
roadmap.description: Your portfolio is everything in design. Build 3 case studies that show your process, not just pretty screens.
steps: Case Study 1: Redesign an existing app — research the problems, propose solutions, design the screens
Case Study 2: Design a new product from scratch — user research, wireframes, prototype, usability testing
Case Study 3: A design challenge or concept project that shows range (mobile app, dashboard, etc.)
Each case study should show your process: research → ideation → wireframes → final design → testing
Build your portfolio website — use Figma to design it, then build with a free tool like Framer or Webflow
Get portfolio feedback from design communities: Dribbble, Behance, ADPList, or r/UXDesign

roadmap.title: Get Hired
roadmap.description: Design hiring is portfolio-first. Lead with your work and your story.
steps: Apply for roles: 'UX Designer', 'UI Designer', 'Product Designer', 'UX Researcher'
Prepare for the design challenge — most interviews include a take-home or whiteboard exercise
Practice presenting your case studies: explain your thinking, not just your screens
Apply to apprenticeship programs: Meta Design Apprenticeship, Google, Microsoft LEAP
Network with designers on ADPList (free mentorship), LinkedIn, and local design meetups
Consider freelance UX work on Toptal or Upwork to build client experience
Start a daily UI challenge (dailyui.co) to keep your skills sharp during the job search

freeResources.name: Google UX Design Certificate
freeResources.description: Google's comprehensive UX design program. Covers research, wireframing, prototyping, and usability testing. Free with Coursera trial.

freeResources.name: Figma for Beginners (Official)
freeResources.description: Learn the industry-standard design tool directly from Figma. Free tool, free tutorials. This is where you'll spend most of your design time.

freeResources.name: Laws of UX
freeResources.description: A beautiful collection of UX design principles backed by psychology research. Bookmark this — you'll reference it in every project.

freeResources.name: ADPList (Free Mentorship)
freeResources.description: Free 1-on-1 mentorship sessions with working designers at top companies. Get portfolio reviews, career advice, and interview prep from the people doing the hiring.

freeResources.name: Daily UI Challenge
freeResources.description: Get a design prompt every day for 100 days. Builds your skills and fills your portfolio with diverse work.

freeResources.name: Nielsen Norman Group Articles
freeResources.description: The gold standard for UX research and best practices. Free articles from the world's leading UX research firm. Essential reading.

title: Product Management
description: Product managers decide what gets built and why. You're the voice of the customer inside the company — you talk to users, analyze data, and work with engineering and design to ship products people love. You don't need to code or design, but you need to understand both worlds. PMs are often called 'the CEO of the product' because you own the strategy, the roadmap, and the results. If you're the person who always asks 'but does anyone actually need this?', you think like a PM.
whatYoullActuallyDo: Talk to customers and users to understand their problems and needs
Write product requirements documents (PRDs) that engineering teams build from
Prioritize features — decide what to build next based on impact and effort
Analyze product metrics: user engagement, retention, conversion, revenue
Run sprint planning with engineering and design — define what gets shipped each cycle
Present product strategy and results to leadership and stakeholders

aiSkills.name: AI-Powered User Research Synthesis
aiSkills.description: Feed user interview transcripts, support tickets, and app reviews into AI to instantly identify the top pain points, feature requests, and trends. What used to take a week of analysis now takes an hour.

aiSkills.name: PRD Generation with AI
aiSkills.description: Describe a feature concept to AI and get a structured product requirements document — complete with user stories, acceptance criteria, edge cases, and success metrics. You refine, AI scaffolds.

aiSkills.name: Competitive Analysis Automation
aiSkills.description: Use AI to research competitors, summarize their features, pricing, and positioning, and identify gaps in the market. Stay ahead without spending days in spreadsheets.

aiSkills.name: Data Analysis & SQL with AI
aiSkills.description: PMs who can pull their own data move faster. Use AI to write SQL queries, analyze product metrics, and generate insights without waiting on the data team.

aiSkills.name: AI-Assisted Prioritization
aiSkills.description: Input your backlog into AI along with your strategy goals and let it help you score and prioritize features using frameworks like RICE, ICE, or MoSCoW.

aiSkills.name: Stakeholder Communication Drafting
aiSkills.description: Generate polished product update emails, launch announcements, and board-ready presentations. AI helps you tailor the message for engineers, executives, or customers.

transferableSkills.name: Customer Empathy
transferableSkills.from: Sales, Customer Service, Healthcare, Social Work
transferableSkills.description: Understanding what people need (not just what they say they want) is the foundation of product management. Your experience listening to customers gives you an edge most PMs lack.

transferableSkills.name: Strategic Thinking
transferableSkills.description: PMs think about the big picture while executing in the details. If you've ever developed a business strategy, planned a campaign, or set organizational priorities, you have PM DNA.

transferableSkills.name: Data-Driven Decision Making
transferableSkills.description: Good PMs back opinions with data. If you're used to making decisions based on numbers, reports, or evidence rather than gut feeling, you'll thrive in product.

transferableSkills.name: Cross-Functional Collaboration
transferableSkills.description: PMs work with everyone — engineering, design, marketing, sales, support. If you've ever been the person connecting different teams or translating between departments, that's product management.

transferableSkills.name: Communication & Influence
transferableSkills.description: PMs lead through influence, not authority. Your ability to persuade, present clearly, and get buy-in from diverse stakeholders is the difference between a feature that ships and one that doesn't.

roadmap.title: Learn Product Thinking
roadmap.description: Understand what product managers actually do and start thinking like one.
steps: Read 'Inspired' by Marty Cagan — the definitive book on product management in tech
Learn product frameworks: Jobs To Be Done (JTBD), product-market fit, lean startup methodology
Study the product development lifecycle: discovery, definition, development, delivery, iteration
Analyze 3 products you use daily — identify their target users, value proposition, and business model
Subscribe to product newsletters: Lenny's Newsletter, Product Hunt Daily, SVPG blog
Watch PM talks on YouTube from Google, Meta, and Airbnb product leaders

roadmap.title: Build Core PM Skills
roadmap.description: Develop the technical and analytical skills that separate great PMs from mediocre ones.
steps: Learn SQL basics — SELECT, WHERE, JOIN, GROUP BY (PMs who can query data move 3x faster)
Study product analytics: learn what metrics matter (DAU, retention, conversion, NPS)
Learn prioritization frameworks: RICE scoring, ICE, MoSCoW, Kano model
Practice writing PRDs — pick a feature for an app you use and write the full spec
Learn wireframing basics in Figma — you don't need to be a designer, but you need to communicate visually
Complete a product management course: Google PM course, Reforge (free content), or Product School talks

roadmap.title: Build Your PM Portfolio
roadmap.description: Create tangible evidence of product thinking. PMs are hired on how they think, not what they've shipped.
steps: Case Study 1: Identify a problem with a real product, research users, propose a solution, and write the PRD
Case Study 2: Do a full product teardown — analyze an app's strategy, metrics, competitive positioning, and improvement opportunities
Case Study 3: Propose a new product or feature from scratch — market research, user personas, MVP definition, go-to-market strategy
Write product thinking blog posts on Medium or Substack — sharing your analysis publicly builds credibility
Volunteer to manage a product at a nonprofit or open source project to get real PM experience
Build a portfolio site or Notion page showcasing your case studies and product thinking

roadmap.title: Get Hired
roadmap.description: PM interviews are unique — they test how you think, not what you know. Prepare accordingly.
steps: Apply for roles: 'Associate Product Manager', 'Product Manager', 'Product Analyst'
Target APM (Associate Product Manager) programs at Google, Meta, Salesforce, and startups
Practice PM interview types: product sense, analytical, execution, and behavioral
Do mock PM interviews on Exponent or with peers — practice out loud, not just in your head
Apply to Salesforce Pathfinder, Microsoft LEAP, and Google apprenticeships
Network with PMs — reach out on LinkedIn for informational interviews and ask about their path
Consider adjacent roles as stepping stones: business analyst, product analyst, or product operations

freeResources.name: Lenny's Newsletter
freeResources.description: The #1 newsletter for product managers. Real-world advice, frameworks, and interviews with PMs from top companies. Free tier available.

freeResources.name: Product School (Free Talks)
freeResources.description: Free webinars and talks from PMs at Google, Meta, Amazon, and Netflix. Learn what the job really looks like from people who do it.

freeResources.name: Exponent PM Interview Prep
freeResources.description: Free PM interview course with practice questions and frameworks. Essential for preparing for product sense and estimation questions.

freeResources.name: SVPG Blog (Marty Cagan)
freeResources.description: Marty Cagan's blog on product management best practices. The gold standard for how top product teams operate. Free and invaluable.

freeResources.name: Reforge (Free Content)
freeResources.description: Deep-dive articles on growth, retention, product strategy, and experimentation from practitioners at top companies.

freeResources.name: SQLBolt
freeResources.description: Learn SQL through hands-on exercises. PMs who can pull their own data don't wait on analysts. This gives you independence.

title: Cybersecurity
description: Cybersecurity professionals protect companies from hackers, data breaches, and digital threats. You're the shield between an organization's data and the people trying to steal it. Every company that uses technology (so, every company) needs security — and there aren't nearly enough people to fill the roles. The cybersecurity talent shortage is massive, which means high demand, strong salaries, and job security. If you're naturally curious, enjoy solving puzzles, and get a thrill from outsmarting bad actors, this career was made for you.
whatYoullActuallyDo: Monitor security alerts and investigate potential threats and breaches
Conduct vulnerability assessments — scan systems to find weaknesses before attackers do
Respond to security incidents: contain threats, investigate root causes, document findings
Implement security controls: firewalls, access management, encryption, endpoint protection
Write security policies and train employees on best practices (phishing awareness, password hygiene)
Perform risk assessments and recommend security improvements to leadership

aiSkills.name: AI-Powered Threat Detection
aiSkills.description: Use AI-enhanced security tools (SIEM platforms, EDR systems) that automatically detect anomalous behavior, flag suspicious activity, and reduce false positives. Let AI handle the noise so you focus on real threats.

aiSkills.name: Automated Log Analysis
aiSkills.description: Feed massive security logs into AI to instantly identify patterns, correlations, and indicators of compromise. What took analysts hours of manual review now surfaces in seconds.

aiSkills.name: AI-Assisted Incident Response
aiSkills.description: Use AI to quickly analyze malware samples, map attack vectors, and generate incident response playbooks. Faster response means less damage.

aiSkills.name: Vulnerability Report Generation
aiSkills.description: Use AI to turn raw scan results into clear, prioritized vulnerability reports with remediation recommendations. Communicate risk to technical and non-technical audiences.

aiSkills.name: Phishing Detection & Analysis
aiSkills.description: AI tools can analyze emails, URLs, and attachments at scale to identify phishing attempts. Train AI models to catch increasingly sophisticated social engineering attacks.

aiSkills.name: Security Policy Drafting
aiSkills.description: Generate security policies, compliance documentation, and employee training materials with AI. Ensure your organization meets regulatory requirements (SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA) faster.

transferableSkills.name: Investigative Mindset
transferableSkills.description: Security analysts are digital detectives. Your ability to follow leads, piece together evidence, and think like an adversary directly translates to threat hunting and incident investigation.

transferableSkills.name: Attention to Detail
transferableSkills.description: A single misconfigured setting can expose an entire company. Your meticulous nature and habit of double-checking everything is exactly what security work demands.

transferableSkills.name: Risk Assessment
transferableSkills.description: Cybersecurity is fundamentally about managing risk. If you've ever evaluated risk likelihood and impact, or created mitigation plans, you already think like a security professional.

transferableSkills.name: Communication Under Pressure
transferableSkills.from: Emergency Services, Healthcare, Military, Customer Service
transferableSkills.description: When a breach happens, you need to stay calm, communicate clearly, and coordinate a response. Your experience working under pressure is invaluable in incident response.

transferableSkills.name: Rule Following & Compliance
transferableSkills.description: Security is full of frameworks, regulations, and standards (NIST, ISO 27001, SOC 2). Your comfort with structured processes and compliance requirements gives you a head start.

roadmap.title: Build Your Foundation
roadmap.description: Learn how computers and networks work. You can't secure what you don't understand.
steps: Learn networking fundamentals: TCP/IP, DNS, HTTP/HTTPS, ports, firewalls (Professor Messer's free videos are excellent)
Understand operating systems: learn basic Linux commands (most security tools run on Linux)
Complete Google Cybersecurity Certificate on Coursera (free with trial)
Learn the CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) — the foundation of all security
Set up a home lab: install VirtualBox, run a Linux VM, and practice basic command-line operations
Study common attack types: phishing, malware, ransomware, SQL injection, DDoS, social engineering

roadmap.title: Get Certified & Go Deeper
roadmap.description: Certifications matter more in cybersecurity than almost any other tech field. Start with the industry standard.
steps: Study for CompTIA Security+ certification — the #1 entry-level security cert (employers love it)
Learn security tools: Wireshark (network analysis), Nmap (scanning), Burp Suite (web security)
Practice on TryHackMe or Hack The Box — hands-on labs that teach security through challenges
Study the NIST Cybersecurity Framework — the standard framework used by most organizations
Learn basic Python scripting for security automation (automating scans, parsing logs)
Understand identity and access management: authentication, authorization, MFA, SSO

roadmap.title: Build Real Experience
roadmap.description: Hands-on experience is everything in security. Build a portfolio that proves you can do the work.
steps: Complete TryHackMe learning paths: SOC Analyst Level 1 or Cyber Defense path
Build a home security lab: set up a SIEM (Security Onion or Wazuh), monitor traffic, detect threats
Participate in Capture the Flag (CTF) competitions — great for learning and your resume
Document everything: write up your lab setup, CTF solutions, and security analyses
Contribute to open-source security projects on GitHub
Get your CompTIA Security+ certification (exam costs ~$400 but opens many doors)

roadmap.title: Get Hired
roadmap.description: The cybersecurity talent gap means companies are hungry for candidates. Position yourself right.
steps: Apply for roles: 'SOC Analyst', 'Security Analyst', 'Information Security Analyst', 'IT Security Specialist'
Target companies with security apprenticeship programs: Cisco, IBM, Google
Apply to Cisco Cybersecurity Apprenticeship and IBM Apprenticeship programs
Join security communities: OWASP local chapter, DEF CON groups, r/cybersecurity
Practice security interview questions: incident response scenarios, network analysis, risk assessment
Network at security conferences: BSides (free/cheap), local ISSA chapters, virtual security meetups
Consider help desk or IT support roles as stepping stones — they build the technical foundation security teams value

freeResources.name: Google Cybersecurity Certificate
freeResources.description: Google's comprehensive cybersecurity program. Covers security fundamentals, network security, Linux, SQL, and incident response. Free with Coursera trial.

freeResources.name: TryHackMe
freeResources.description: Learn cybersecurity through interactive, gamified labs. Start with the 'Complete Beginner' path. Many rooms are free. The best way to learn security by doing.

freeResources.name: Professor Messer (CompTIA Security+)
freeResources.description: Free, comprehensive video course covering everything on the CompTIA Security+ exam. Professor Messer is the gold standard for cert prep.

freeResources.name: Hack The Box
freeResources.description: Hands-on cybersecurity training with real-world scenarios. Free tier includes practice machines and challenges. Great for building practical skills.

freeResources.name: OWASP Web Security Testing Guide
freeResources.description: The definitive guide to web application security testing. Free, open-source, and constantly updated. Essential knowledge for any security role.

freeResources.name: Cybrary
freeResources.description: Free cybersecurity courses covering network security, ethical hacking, incident response, and more. Includes hands-on virtual labs.
